value: Headline: The Hormuz war premium is bleeding out fast — energy officials now call tanker traffic through the strait 'back to normal' — yet a brutal week-ending June 12 EIA print (crude -8.3 mb, Cushing slipping to 20.0 mb) is keeping a hard floor under flat price. WTI settled Friday at $76.54, up $0.69 on the session, with Brent at $80.59 (+$0.74). The tug-of-war is clean: easing geopolitics on one side, a tank-bottom Cushing and 96.7% refinery runs on the other. Crude & products: The latest weekly EIA report, for the week ending June 12, 2026, showed a commercial crude draw of 8.3 mb to 418.2 mb, with total petroleum stocks down 9.4 mb to 780.5 mb. Gasoline drew 0.9 mb to 214.2 mb and jet fell 1.1 mb to 45.0 mb, while distillate bucked the trend with a 1.0 mb build to 103.1 mb. Most critically, the report showed Cushing fell 1.6 mb to 20.0 mb — already below the ~25 mb operational tank-bottom, and down 5.79 mb over five weeks. Refinery utilization climbed 1.4 pp to 96.7%, with PADD V the headline mover, snapping up 8.10 pp to 93.4% as West Coast units came back online. Refining margins: Margins remain robust off the strong run rates. The 3-2-1 crack sits at $49.11, with the distillate crack the standout at $55.79 versus gasoline at $45.77 and jet at $42.32. The firm distillate crack squares with the 1.0 mb diesel build but tightening implied demand into summer; RBOB settled at $2.9122/gal (+$0.01) and ULSD at $3.1508/gal (+$0.07), the heavier ULSD gain reflecting that diesel-led margin strength. With runs already at 96.7%, refiners have little spare capacity to chase these cracks higher into July. Curve structure: The prompt structure stays backwardated and supportive — WTI M1-M2 at $0.92 and the M1-M12 at $6.54, with Brent M1-M12 at $6.37. That backwardation is consistent with the sub-floor Cushing dynamic: scarcity at the delivery hub bids the front. The Brent-WTI spread, however, has compressed to just $4.05 — a narrow arb that weakens the US export incentive, encouraging barrels to stay home and pressuring WTI relative to Brent. To re-open the export window and relieve domestic length, WTI would need to fall (or Brent rise) to re-widen that spread back toward $7. News & geopolitics: The dominant story is de-escalation: the US energy secretary forecast further price declines and confirmed Hormuz traffic is 'back to normal,' while separate analysis underscores that despite Iran's closure rhetoric, oil keeps flowing through the strait — both bearish for the war premium. Indian refiners are leaning into the recovery, lifting Russian and UAE imports ahead of normalized Hormuz flows, a sign physical buyers expect cheaper barrels. Downstream, the unwind is visible globally — Australian petrol has fallen below pre-war levels, Taiwan's CPC and Philippine retailers are flagging rollbacks of up to P11/L on diesel next week, and India's oil minister insists pump prices won't spike despite the volatility. The takeaway for today: the geopolitical bid is deflating faster than the physical tightness shown in the EIA data, leaving Cushing's sub-floor stress and 96.7% runs as the real bullish anchors against a softening macro tape.
